title: 2 Corinthians 12
subtitle: Visions, the Thorn, and Power in Weakness

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — "The Third Heaven, the Thorn, and Sufficient Grace"

heb.paragraph: The phrase skolops tē sarki (‘thorn in the flesh,’ v. 7) is one of the NT’s most debated expressions. A skolops is a sharp stake or splinter—something that pierces and produces persistent pain. Proposed identifications include a physical ailment (eye disease, malaria, epilepsy), a spiritual struggle (temptation, satanic opposition), or a personal adversary (the super-apostles, persecuting opponents). Paul calls it ‘a messenger of Satan’ (angelos Satana), indicating that it serves Satan’s purposes (to torment) while being permitted by God (to prevent conceit). The divine refusal to remove it (v. 9) transforms it from affliction into pedagogical gift.

heb.gloss: it is sufficient, it is enough
heb.paragraph: The verb arkei (‘is sufficient,’ v. 9) is Christ’s response to Paul’s thrice-repeated prayer: ‘My grace is sufficient for you.’ The divine reply does not remove the thorn but reframes it: the sufficiency of grace means that weakness is not an obstacle to be overcome but the condition in which divine power ‘is made perfect’ (teleitai). The passive voice (teleitai, ‘is perfected’) indicates that the perfecting of power in weakness is God’s own work, not a human achievement.

refs.note: Jesus’ threefold Gethsemane prayer provides the christological precedent for Paul’s threefold prayer for the thorn’s removal. In both cases, the divine answer is not removal but transformation of suffering into redemptive purpose.

The jars-of-clay theology of ch. 4 provides the framework for the power-in-weakness declaration: divine treasure in fragile vessels demonstrates that ‘this all-surpassing power is from God and not from us.’
Paul’s earlier admission of ‘weakness and fear and much trembling’ in Corinth is now interpreted christologically: his weakness was the condition for the Spirit’s powerful demonstration.

notes.note: Paul reports a visionary experience of extraordinary intensity—caught up to the ‘third heaven’ (the highest heaven, God’s dwelling place) and ‘paradise.’ Yet he refuses to exploit it: he speaks in the third person, withholds the content, and refuses to boast except about his weaknesses (v. 5). The super-apostles apparently paraded their visions as credentials; Paul has visions but refuses to use them that way.

The thorn in the flesh is Satan’s instrument but God’s pedagogy: it prevents the spiritual pride that extraordinary revelation might produce. Paul’s threefold prayer mirrors Gethsemane: persistence in prayer, submission to the divine answer. Christ’s response—‘My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness’—is the theological climax of the entire letter. Divine power does not compensate for weakness or eliminate it but is perfected within it.

Paul’s response to Christ’s answer is not resignation but exultation: ‘I will boast all the more gladly about my weaknesses, so that Christ’s power may rest on me’ (v. 9b). The verb ‘rest’ (episkēnoō, literally ‘pitch a tent over’) echoes the Shekinah glory of the tabernacle: Christ’s power ‘tabernacles’ over the weak apostle. The paradoxical conclusion: ‘When I am weak, then I am strong’ (v. 10).

Calvin reads the thorn-in-the-flesh passage as the definitive statement of the relationship between grace and suffering. The thorn is simultaneously a satanic affliction and a divine gift: Satan intends torment; God intends humility. Calvin applies this to all believers: afflictions that seem destructive are often God’s instruments for preventing spiritual pride and deepening dependence on grace. The ‘sufficient grace’ declaration (v. 9) is, for Calvin, the Christian’s charter of endurance: no trial exceeds the sufficiency of divine grace, and weakness is the very condition in which grace operates most powerfully.

The NET note discusses the ‘third heaven’: in Jewish cosmology, the heavens were typically divided into three (atmospheric, celestial, divine dwelling) or seven layers. The ‘third heaven’ is the highest—God’s own presence.

The NET note on the thorn’s identity surveys the major proposals without reaching a definitive conclusion. The note observes that Paul’s deliberate vagueness may be intentional: the specific affliction matters less than its theological function.

The NET note on ‘power is made perfect in weakness’ (hē dynamis en astheneia teleitai) explains the passive teleitai: divine power reaches its fullest expression precisely in the condition of human weakness, not despite it.

Fee provides the most sustained Pentecostal engagement with this passage. He affirms the reality of Paul’s visionary experience while insisting that Paul’s refusal to exploit it is paradigmatic: genuine spiritual experience does not require public display. Fee reads the ‘power in weakness’ declaration as the definitive refutation of triumphalistic Christianity in any form: the Spirit’s power is not demonstrated by eliminating weakness but by operating within it. Fee identifies the thorn tentatively as a recurring physical ailment, but emphasises that the theological point transcends the diagnosis.

Harris provides the most comprehensive treatment of the passage, addressing the third-heaven cosmology, the thorn’s identity, and the ‘sufficient grace’ christophany. He argues that the vision (vv. 2–4) and the thorn (v. 7) are deliberately paired: the extraordinary privilege necessitates the humbling affliction. The divine response (v. 9) is, for Harris, the hermeneutical key to the entire letter: divine power and human weakness are not in tension but in symbiosis. The verb episkēnoō (v. 9b, ‘to pitch a tent/tabernacle’) is theologically loaded: Christ’s power ‘tabernacles’ over Paul just as God’s glory ‘tabernacled’ over Israel in the wilderness (Exod 40:34–35). The weak apostle becomes the new tabernacle of divine presence.

hist.context: Paul continues the fool’s speech with the most extraordinary visionary experience in his letters: a journey to the ‘third heaven’ / ‘paradise’ (vv. 2–4) fourteen years earlier. Yet he refuses to boast about the vision itself, speaking in the third person (‘I know a man in Christ’) and withholding its content (‘inexpressible things, things that no one is permitted to tell,’ v. 4). The thorn in the flesh (v. 7) was given to prevent conceit—a divine corrective to the spiritual danger of extraordinary revelation. Paul’s prayer for its removal was denied three times (v. 8), echoing Jesus’ Gethsemane prayer (Mark 14:36–39). Christ’s answer (v. 9) is the theological summit of the letter and arguably the most important statement in all of Paul’s writings on the relationship between divine power and human weakness.

sections.header: Verses 11–18 — "Signs of an Apostle and Financial Integrity"

heb.paragraph: The phrase sēmeia tou apostolou (‘signs of an apostle,’ v. 12) is ambiguous: does it mean ‘signs that authenticate an apostle’ (miraculous proofs) or ‘signs that characterise an apostle’ (the suffering-and-weakness pattern)? The immediate context mentions ‘signs, wonders and miracles’ (v. 12b), suggesting Paul acknowledges performing miraculous deeds. Yet the broader context of chs. 10–12 has redefined apostolic credentials as weakness, not power. The phrase likely encompasses both: genuine apostles perform signs but are ultimately recognised by their cruciform character.

refs.note: Paul’s acknowledgement that Christ accomplished things ‘through me’ by ‘signs, wonders and miracles’ parallels the signs-of-an-apostle claim here.
Paul’s earlier defence of his right to financial support (which he voluntarily waived) provides the context for the ongoing financial controversy addressed here.

notes.note: Paul’s self-critique—‘I have made a fool of myself’—is combined with a rebuke: ‘you drove me to it.’ The Corinthians should have defended Paul rather than forcing him into self-commendation. The apostolic signs (v. 12) confirm his credentials, but the ironic ‘forgive me this wrong!’ (v. 13)—the ‘wrong’ of not burdening them financially—is Paul’s most sarcastic moment.

Paul’s parental metaphor (v. 14: ‘children should not have to save up for their parents’) reveals the depth of his pastoral relationship: he is father, not hireling. His willingness to ‘spend and be expended’ (v. 15) for the Corinthians is the language of self-sacrificial love. The financial integrity defence (vv. 16–18) anticipates any charge of indirect exploitation through Titus.

Calvin reads the ‘signs of an apostle’ as encompassing both miraculous attestation and cruciform character: the true apostle is recognised by both supernatural confirmation and Christlike suffering. Calvin applies Paul’s financial transparency to church practice: ministers must be above reproach in financial matters, not merely innocent but demonstrably so.

The NET note discusses the triad ‘signs, wonders and miracles’ (sēmeia, terata, dynameis): this is the standard NT formula for miraculous attestation (cf. Acts 2:22; Heb 2:4). The note observes that Paul’s acknowledgement of performing miracles is surprisingly understated given the letter’s context.

Fee notes the irony of v. 13: Paul’s ‘wrong’ was love expressed through self-support. The super-apostles exploit the Corinthians financially; Paul refuses to take from them. Fee reads the signs-of-an-apostle claim as genuine but secondary: the primary apostolic credential is the cross-shaped life, not miraculous power.

Harris argues that the ‘signs of an apostle’ (v. 12) should be understood as including but not limited to miracles: they encompass the entire pattern of Paul’s ministry—patience (hypomonē), signs, wonders, and miracles. Harris reads the financial-integrity section (vv. 14–18) as Paul’s pre-emptive defence before the third visit: he addresses every possible accusation of financial misconduct, directly or through intermediaries.

hist.context: Paul concludes the fool’s speech by acknowledging that the Corinthians ‘drove him to it’ (v. 11): they should have commended him rather than forcing self-defence. He affirms that apostolic signs were performed among them (v. 12) and addresses the financial accusation with biting irony: ‘How were you inferior to the other churches, except that I was never a burden to you? Forgive me this wrong!’ (v. 13). The planned third visit (v. 14) is motivated by love, not exploitation: Paul wants them, not their possessions. The financial integrity section (vv. 16–18) pre-empts the charge that Paul used Titus as a financial intermediary to exploit the Corinthians indirectly.

sections.header: Verses 19–21 — "Fear of What Paul May Find"

heb.paragraph: The noun akatharsia (impurity) in v. 21 heads a vice triad (impurity, sexual sin, debauchery) that echoes the moral concerns of 1 Corinthians 5–6. Paul fears that his third visit will reveal ongoing moral failure among members who have ‘not repented.’ The term’s placement at the letter’s end suggests that the Corinthian moral crisis was not fully resolved by the tearful letter and repentance of ch. 7.

refs.note: Paul’s earlier confrontation with sexual immorality in Corinth provides the background for his continuing concern about ‘impurity, sexual sin, and debauchery’ (v. 21).
Paul’s vice list in Galatians (‘acts of the flesh’) includes many of the same terms, confirming that these are characteristic Pauline categories of moral failure.

notes.note: Paul’s dual fear—of finding persistent sin and of being humbled by God because of it—reveals the pastoral weight of apostolic ministry. The vice catalogue (vv. 20–21) addresses both relational sins (discord, jealousy, factions) and sexual sins (impurity, debauchery), suggesting that both remain problems in Corinth.

Calvin reads Paul’s fear as evidence of genuine pastoral concern: the minister who does not grieve over his congregation’s sins has ceased to be a true shepherd. The comprehensive vice catalogue warns that repentance (ch. 7) does not guarantee permanent reform: ongoing vigilance is necessary.
The NET note on ‘my God will humble me’ (tapeinōsē me) explains that Paul anticipates being humbled by the shame of finding unrepentant sin in a church he has personally planted and nurtured.

Fee reads the vice catalogue as evidence that the Corinthian problems were not fully resolved: sexual immorality and factional rivalry persist. The upcoming third visit will test whether the Corinthians have genuinely repented or merely offered temporary compliance.

Harris notes the structural function of these verses: they transition from the fool’s speech (11:1–12:10) to the final warnings of ch. 13. Paul’s fear of finding unrepentant sin (v. 21) prepares for the stern tone of the closing chapter.

hist.context: Paul concludes the polemical section with a sobering confession of fear: he fears that his upcoming visit will bring mutual disappointment—he may not find the Corinthians as he wants them, and they may not find him as they want him (v. 20). The catalogue of potential sins (discord, jealousy, outbursts of anger, factions, slander, gossip, arrogance, disorder, v. 20; impurity, sexual sin, debauchery, v. 21) echoes both 1 Corinthians and the earlier chapters of this letter. Paul’s concern is genuine, not rhetorical: the third visit may require the exercise of the authority he has been defending.

themes.note: Faith and Sovereignty dominate as Paul recounts the vision, the thorn, and Christ’s sufficient-grace response. Mercy scores highly through the grace declaration.

rows.label: vv.1–6
rows.text: Third heaven vision: extraordinary experience, third-person restraint

rows.label: vv.7–10
rows.text: Thorn in the flesh: threefold prayer, sufficient grace, power in weakness

rows.label: vv.11–13
rows.text: Signs of an apostle and the ‘wrong’ of free ministry

rows.label: vv.14–18
rows.text: Third visit planned: parental love, financial integrity

rows.label: vv.19–21
rows.text: Fear of unrepentant sin: vice catalogue

lit.note: Chapter 12 contains the letter’s theological summit: the power-in-weakness declaration (v. 9). The thorn-in-the-flesh narrative demonstrates that divine grace does not remove suffering but transforms it into the very condition of power.

hebtext.note: The phrase skolops tē sarki (‘thorn in the flesh,’ v. 7) is one of the NT’s most debated expressions. A skolops is a sharp stake or splinter—something that pierces and produces persistent pain. The noun akatharsia (impurity) in v. 21 heads a vice triad (impurity, sexual sin, debauchery) that echoes the moral concerns of 1 Corinthians 5–6. ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

discourse.thesis: Paul's thorn in the flesh and God's sufficient grace — power is made perfect in weakness.

nodes.text: Paul knows a man caught up to the third heaven, to paradise — whether in body or out, God knows. He heard inexpressible things. But he will not boast except in weakness.

A thorn in the flesh was given — a messenger of Satan to keep Paul from being conceited. Three times he pleaded for removal. God's answer: 'My grace is sufficient; my power is made perfect in weakness.' So Paul boasts in weaknesses.
Paul has been a fool — the Corinthians forced him. He is not inferior to the super-apostles. The signs of an apostle were performed among them.
Paul prepares to visit a third time. He seeks them, not their possessions. He fears finding quarreling, jealousy, disorder — and having to mourn over those who have not repented.
